### 💬 Copilot

Hey Justin! Squad v0.5.2 here. No `team.md` yet — let me cast your team.

**Project:** BitFocus Companion module for Zoom OSC/ISO (TypeScript, OSC protocol, actions/feedbacks/presets/variables)
**Universe:** Lord of the Rings ✅

Here's your proposed team:

```
🏗️  Gandalf   — Lead              Architecture, decisions, code review
🔧  Samwise   — Integration Dev   OSC protocol, actions, feedbacks, state machine
⚛️  Pippin    — UI Dev            Presets, variables, Companion UI config
🧪  Eowyn     — Tester            Tests, quality, edge cases
📋  Scribe    — (silent)          Memory, decisions, session logs
🔄  Ralph     — (monitor)         Work queue, backlog, keep-alive
```

<sub>⏱️ 4m 9s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Good call. The Companion API (actions, feedbacks, variables, presets) is really backend integration work anyway. Here's the revised team:

```
🏗️  Gandalf   — Lead              Architecture, decisions, code review
🔧  Samwise   — Integration Dev   OSC protocol, actions, feedbacks, state machine
📐  Merry     — Module Dev        Actions definitions, feedbacks, variables, presets (Companion API standards)
🧪  Eowyn     — Tester            Tests, quality, edge cases
📋  Scribe    — (silent)          Memory, decisions, session logs
🔄  Ralph     — (monitor)         Work queue, backlog, keep-alive
```

Merry handles the Companion-side API layer — making sure all the action/feedback/variable/preset definitions are correct and standards-compliant, while Samwise owns the OSC plumbing.

# What We're Focused On

Getting started. Updated by coordinator at session start.

# Ceremonies

> Team meetings that happen before or after work. Each squad configures their own.

## Design Review

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Trigger** | auto |
| **When** | before |
| **Condition** | multi-agent task involving 2+ agents modifying shared systems |
| **Facilitator** | lead |
| **Participants** | all-relevant |
| **Time budget** | focused |
| **Enabled** | ✅ yes |

**Agenda:**
1. Review the task and requirements
2. Agree on interfaces and contracts between components
3. Identify risks and edge cases
4. Assign action items

---

## Retrospective

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Trigger** | auto |
| **When** | after |
| **Condition** | build failure, test failure, or reviewer rejection |
| **Facilitator** | lead |
| **Participants** | all-involved |
| **Time budget** | focused |
| **Enabled** | ✅ yes |

**Agenda:**
1. What happened? (facts only)
2. Root cause analysis
3. What should change?
4. Action items for next iteration
---

{
	"description": "🏗️ Gandalf: Review project structure",

	"prompt": "You are Gandalf, the Lead on this project.\n\nYOUR CHARTER:\n# Gandalf — Lead\n\n## Identity\nYou are Gandalf, the Lead on this project. You set architectural direction, make scope decisions, review code, and ensure the team is building the right thing the right way.\n\n## Responsibilities\n- Architectural decisions and design reviews\n- Code review across all modules\n- Scope and priority decisions\n- Coordinating multi-agent work\n- Reviewing and approving significant changes\n\n## Boundaries\n- Do NOT merge PRs without review\n- Do NOT unilaterally change another agent's domain without discussion\n- Surface blockers to the user rather than guessing past them\n\n## Output Standards\n- Decisions go to `.squad/decisions/inbox/gandalf-{slug}.md`\n- History learnings appended to `.squad/agents/gandalf/history.md`\n- Code changes are minimal and surgical — no refactors without justification\n\nTEAM ROOT: /Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso\nAll `.squad/` paths are relative to this root.\n\nRead .squad/agents/gandalf/history.md (your project knowledge).\nRead .squad/decisions.md (team decisions to respect).\nIf .squad/identity/wisdom.md exists, read it before starting work.\nIf .squad/identity/now.md exists, read it at spawn time.\n\n**Requested by:** Justin James\n\nThe user says: \"Review the project structure and tell me what's in good shape and what needs attention.\"\n\nDo a thorough review of the project. Specifically:\n\n1. Read `package.json` — check dependencies, scripts, version, module metadata\n2. Read `tsconfig.json` and `tsconfig.build.json` — TypeScript config health\n3. Read `src/index.ts` — module entry point, how it registers with Companion\n4. Read `src/config.ts` — config schema\n5. Read `src/osc.ts` — OSC connection handling\n6. Read `src/actions.ts` (and list `src/actions/` directory if it exists)\n7. Read `src/feedback.ts` and `src/feedback-state-machine.ts`\n8. Read `src/variables/` directory listing\n9. Read `src/presets.ts` (and list `src/presets/` directory if it exists)\n10. Read `src/upgrades/` directory listing\n11. Check if any test files exist anywhere in the repo\n12. Check `README.md` for documentation state\n13. Check `eslint.config.mjs` for linting setup\n\nFor each area, assess:\n- What's in good shape (clear, correct, well-structured)\n- What needs attention (missing, broken, unclear, risky)\n- What's a nice-to-have improvement vs. a real concern\n\nDo NOT make any code changes. Observation and assessment only.\n\nAfter reviewing, write your findings to `.squad/decisions/inbox/gandalf-project-review.md` in this format:\n```\n### Project Structure Review — {date}\n**By:** Gandalf\n**Status:** Assessment only\n\n#### ✅ In Good Shape\n- ...\n\n#### ⚠️ Needs Attention\n- ...\n\n#### 🔴 Concerns\n- ...\n\n#### Recommended next actions\n- ...\n```\n\nAlso append key findings to .squad/agents/gandalf/history.md under \"## Learnings\".\n\n⚠️ OUTPUT: Report outcomes in human terms. Write a clear, direct assessment for Justin James. No tool internals.\n\n⚠️ RESPONSE ORDER: After ALL tool calls, write a 2-3 paragraph plain text summary as your FINAL output. No tool calls after this summary.\n",
	"model": "claude-opus-4.6"
}

<sub>⏱️ 8922m 16s</sub>

### ✅ `read_agent`

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
	"agent_id": "agent-0",
	"wait": true,
	"timeout": 300
}
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>6 lines</summary>

```
Agent completed. agent_id: agent-0, agent_type: general-purpose, status: completed, description: 🏗️ Gandalf: Review project structure, elapsed: 138s, model: claude-opus-4.6, duration: 138s

Result:
Justin — here's the executive summary.

**The good news:** This module is in solid working shape. Build and lint both pass clean, the architecture is well-organized with clear separation across 50+ files (28 action modules, 18 preset modules, dedicated feedback, variables, config, and upgrade layers), and the README provides thorough end-user documentation. The toolchain (TypeScript, ESLint, Prettier, Husky) is modern and properly configured. For a Companion module of this complexity, the code organization is genuinely good.

**The concerns:** There are three real bugs to fix — `UpgradeV2ToV3` is registered twice in the entry point, two config field IDs are duplicated (which could cause rendering issues), and `destroy()` assigns arrays to object-typed properties. Beyond bugs, the biggest structural risk is `osc.ts` at 1,040 lines — it's doing connection management, message parsing, state updates, and ZoomISO polling all in one file. And the elephant in the room: zero test coverage across the entire project. For a module this size, that's the single biggest risk for ongoing development.

**My recommended priority order:** (1) Fix the three bugs — they're small, surgical, and Merry can knock them out quickly. (2) Have Eowyn stand up a test framework and start with unit tests on the pure utility functions. (3) Plan a phased decomposition of `osc.ts` with Samwise — not urgent, but it's where the architectural debt lives. Full details are in `.squad/decisions/inbox/gandalf-project-review.md`.
```

</details>

---

# Team Decisions

Canonical decision ledger. Append-only. Updated by Scribe from `.squad/decisions/inbox/`.

---

## 2026-02-21: Team formed

**By:** Justin James
**Decision:** Hired a Lord of the Rings–cast team for companion-module-zoom-osc-iso.
**Roster:** Gandalf (Lead), Samwise (Integration Dev), Merry (Module Dev), Eowyn (Tester), Scribe, Ralph.
**Rationale:** BitFocus handles the UI layer; this team focuses on OSC protocol integration, Companion API definitions, and quality.

---

## 2026-02-28: Project Structure Review — Technical Findings

**By:** Gandalf (Lead)
**Status:** Assessment complete

### Strengths

- Build & lint toolchain: passes cleanly, zero errors or warnings
- Module architecture: clean separation — actions, feedbacks, presets, variables, OSC, upgrades in dedicated files/directories
- Actions layer: 28 files, each scoped to single domain; clear type-safe aggregation pattern
- Presets layer: 18 files covering major Zoom workflows; clean aggregation
- Feedback system: comprehensive multi-state with icon overlays, thorough state machine covering all 16 boolean combinations
- Config: well-structured with input validation, regex constraints, sensible defaults
- Upgrade scripts: 4 scripts properly registered, covering v2→v3 migration and config additions
- TypeScript/ESLint: proper configs, Husky + lint-staged pre-commit hooks, developer tooling solid
- Documentation: thorough README with workflow patterns, HELP.md for in-app help

### Bugs (3 identified)

1. **Duplicate upgrade registration** (`index.ts:191-192`): `UpgradeV2ToV3` registered twice in `runEntrypoint()` upgrade array. Likely idempotent but unintentional.
2. **Duplicate config field IDs** (`config.ts`):
   - `id: 'socialStreamNote'` on lines 128 and 183
   - `id: 'actionPresetAndFeedbackSyncNote'` on lines 206 and 221
   - Could cause Companion config rendering issues.
3. **Type mismatches in `destroy()` method** (`index.ts:144-153`): Object-typed properties assigned `[]` instead of `{}`. TypeScript doesn't catch this due to `disableVariableValidation`, but intent is wrong.

### Code Quality Concerns

- **`require()` in TS files**: `feedback-state-machine.ts:4` (`./images`), `osc.ts:30` (osc package). Local module should use ESM.
- **Orphan file**: `src/v2CommandsToUpgradeTov3.ts` at root; duplicate logic in `src/upgrades/` version.
- **`no-explicit-any` disabled globally**: ESLint config disables this rule. `osc.ts` and `utils.ts` use `any` widely, eroding type safety in critical paths.

### Architectural Concerns

- **Zero test coverage**: No test files across entire project (~4,500+ lines of source, 50+ files). tsconfig.build.json shows test patterns were planned.
- **`osc.ts` monolith**: 1,040 lines handling connection management, message parsing, all Zoom OSC response handling, variable updates, group/gallery management, ZoomISO polling. Single highest-risk file; hardest to safely refactor.

### Recommended Actions

1. **Quick wins (Merry)**: Fix duplicate `UpgradeV2ToV3` registration, duplicate config IDs, remove orphan file
2. **Type safety (Merry)**: Fix `destroy()` to use `{}` instead of `[]`; convert `./images` require to ESM import
3. **Testing foundation (Eowyn)**: Set up vitest; start with unit tests for `utils.ts` helpers and action/feedback callbacks (pure functions)
4. **Long-term (Samwise)**: Plan phased `osc.ts` decomposition (connection, message router, response handlers) — not urgent but core architectural debt
5. **Optional**: Re-enable `no-explicit-any` as warning; gradually type `any` usages in `udpPort` and OSC response handlers
<exited with exit code 0>
```

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 9744m 29s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Now I have everything. Here's the full picture:

---

## 2. Duplicate Config Field IDs

Two IDs are used twice, each time for a completely _different_ content block:

**`socialStreamNote` (lines 128 & 183)**

- Line 128: The Social Stream Ninja integration description banner
- Line 183: Completely different section — "Advanced - Optional Performance Tweaks" header

**`actionPresetAndFeedbackSyncNote` (lines 206 & 221)**

- Line 206: Description for the "Enable Variable For Participants" checkbox
- Line 221: Completely different — description for the "Sync Action/Presets/Feedbacks" checkbox

These are NOT reuses of the same banner — they are different sections that accidentally share IDs. Companion could silently ignore or misrender one if it deduplicates by field ID. The fix is a 2-line rename:

- Line 183: `id: 'socialStreamNote'` → `id: 'advancedPerformanceTweaksNote'`
- Line 206: `id: 'actionPresetAndFeedbackSyncNote'` → `id: 'enableVariablesForParticipantsNote'`

---

## 3. `destroy()` Type Mismatch — Full Detail

Three properties are declared as **objects** (`= {}`) but `destroy()` resets them to **`[]`** (empty array):

| Property               | Declared type                   | Declared default | Reset to |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------- | -------- |
| `ZoomAudioLevelData`   | `ZoomAudioLevelDataInterface`   | `{}`             | `[]` ❌  |
| `ZoomAudioRoutingData` | `ZoomAudioRoutingDataInterface` | `{}`             | `[]` ❌  |
| `ZoomUserOffline`      | `ZoomUserOfflineInterface`      | `{}`             | `[]` ❌  |

In TypeScript strict mode this would be a compile error. Currently it compiles because strict checks aren't fully enabled, but at runtime the module thinks it destroyed the state with an empty object but actually left an array — which could cause silent failures if any downstream code checks `Object.keys()` or iterates with `for...in` expecting an object.

The fix is 3 characters per line: change `[]` → `{}` for those three properties in destroy().

---

**Decision needed from you:**

- Should Merry fix the 3 `destroy()` lines (`[]` → `{}`) and the 2 duplicate config IDs? Both are tiny, safe, and correct-the-record fixes.

---

| Skill                 | Covers                                             |

| `companion-config`    | Config field types, validation, `configUpdated`    |

$ cat /Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso/.squad/skills/make-skill-template/SKILL.md && echo "---BOUNDARY---" && cat /Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso/.squad/skills/companion-action-file-pattern/SKILL.md

<details>
<summary>390 lines</summary>

````
---
name: make-skill-template
description: 'Create new Agent Skills for GitHub Copilot from prompts or by duplicating this template. Use when asked to "create a skill", "make a new skill", "scaffold a skill", or when building specialized AI capabilities with bundled resources. Generates SKILL.md files with proper frontmatter, directory structure, and optional scripts/references/assets folders.'
---

# Make Skill Template

A meta-skill for creating new Agent Skills. Use this skill when you need to scaffold a new skill folder, generate a SKILL.md file, or help users understand the Agent Skills specification.

## When to Use This Skill

- User asks to "create a skill", "make a new skill", or "scaffold a skill"
- User wants to add a specialized capability to their GitHub Copilot setup
- User needs help structuring a skill with bundled resources
- User wants to duplicate this template as a starting point

## Prerequisites

- Understanding of what the skill should accomplish
- A clear, keyword-rich description of capabilities and triggers
- Knowledge of any bundled resources needed (scripts, references, assets, templates)

## Creating a New Skill

### Step 1: Create the Skill Directory

Create a new folder with a lowercase, hyphenated name:

```
skills/<skill-name>/
└── SKILL.md          # Required
```

### Step 2: Generate SKILL.md with Frontmatter

Every skill requires YAML frontmatter with `name` and `description`:

```yaml
---
name: <skill-name>
description: '<What it does>. Use when <specific triggers, scenarios, keywords users might say>.'
---
```

#### Frontmatter Field Requirements

| Field           | Required | Constraints                                                                |
| --------------- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `name`          | **Yes**  | 1-64 chars, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens only, must match folder name |
| `description`   | **Yes**  | 1-1024 chars, must describe WHAT it does AND WHEN to use it                |
| `license`       | No       | License name or reference to bundled LICENSE.txt                           |
| `compatibility` | No       | 1-500 chars, environment requirements if needed                            |
| `metadata`      | No       | Key-value pairs for additional properties                                  |
| `allowed-tools` | No       | Space-delimited list of pre-approved tools (experimental)                  |

#### Description Best Practices

**CRITICAL**: The `description` is the PRIMARY mechanism for automatic skill discovery. Include:

1. **WHAT** the skill does (capabilities)
2. **WHEN** to use it (triggers, scenarios, file types)
3. **Keywords** users might mention in prompts

**Good example:**

```yaml
description: 'Toolkit for testing local web applications using Playwright. Use when asked to verify frontend functionality, debug UI behavior, capture browser screenshots, or view browser console logs. Supports Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit.'
```

**Poor example:**

```yaml
description: 'Web testing helpers'
```

### Step 3: Write the Skill Body

After the frontmatter, add markdown instructions. Recommended sections:

| Section                     | Purpose                         |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| `# Title`                   | Brief overview                  |
| `## When to Use This Skill` | Reinforces description triggers |
| `## Prerequisites`          | Required tools, dependencies    |
| `## Step-by-Step Workflows` | Numbered steps for tasks        |
| `## Troubleshooting`        | Common issues and solutions     |
| `## References`             | Links to bundled docs           |

### Step 4: Add Optional Directories (If Needed)

| Folder        | Purpose                            | When to Use                         |
| ------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `scripts/`    | Executable code (Python, Bash, JS) | Automation that performs operations |
| `references/` | Documentation agent reads          | API references, schemas, guides     |
| `assets/`     | Static files used AS-IS            | Images, fonts, templates            |
| `templates/`  | Starter code agent modifies        | Scaffolds to extend                 |

## Example: Complete Skill Structure

```
my-awesome-skill/
├── SKILL.md                    # Required instructions
├── LICENSE.txt                 # Optional license file
├── scripts/
│   └── helper.py               # Executable automation
├── references/
│   ├── api-reference.md        # Detailed docs
│   └── examples.md             # Usage examples
├── assets/
│   └── diagram.png             # Static resources
└── templates/
    └── starter.ts              # Code scaffold
```

## Quick Start: Duplicate This Template

1. Copy the `make-skill-template/` folder
2. Rename to your skill name (lowercase, hyphens)
3. Update `SKILL.md`:
   - Change `name:` to match folder name
   - Write a keyword-rich `description:`
   - Replace body content with your instructions
4. Add bundled resources as needed
5. Validate with `npm run skill:validate`

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Folder name is lowercase with hyphens
- [ ] `name` field matches folder name exactly
- [ ] `description` is 10-1024 characters
- [ ] `description` explains WHAT and WHEN
- [ ] `description` is wrapped in single quotes
- [ ] Body content is under 500 lines
- [ ] Bundled assets are under 5MB each

## Troubleshooting

| Issue                    | Solution                                                 |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Skill not discovered     | Improve description with more keywords and triggers      |
| Validation fails on name | Ensure lowercase, no consecutive hyphens, matches folder |
| Description too short    | Add capabilities, triggers, and keywords                 |
| Assets not found         | Use relative paths from skill root                       |

## References

- Agent Skills official spec: <https://agentskills.io/specification>
---BOUNDARY---
---
name: companion-action-file-pattern
description: 'Teaches the multi-file action pattern used in split-file Companion modules. Use when asked to add a new action category, create an action file, register actions in an aggregator, or extend the actions layer of a Companion module that splits actions across multiple files with a GetActions aggregator.'
confidence: high
---

# Companion Action File Pattern

This module splits action definitions across many files (one per category), then aggregates them in a single `actions.ts` that calls `setActionDefinitions()`. This skill documents the exact structure and wiring required to add a new action category.

## When to Use This Skill

- Adding a new category of actions to a Companion module with split action files
- Creating a new `src/actions/action-{category}.ts` file
- Registering a new action file in the aggregator (`actions.ts`)
- Understanding the TypeScript types and export shape for action files

## Pattern Overview

```
src/
  actions.ts                        ← aggregator (imports + combines all categories)
  actions/
    action-{category-a}.ts          ← one file per action category
    action-{category-b}.ts
    action-{category-c}.ts
    action-utils.ts                 ← shared helpers used by action files
```

`index.ts` calls:

```typescript
this.setActionDefinitions(GetActions(this))
```

`GetActions()` (in `actions.ts`) calls each category's `GetActions{Category}(instance)`, collects the typed objects, spreads them into one combined object, and returns it.

---

## Pattern 1 — The Action File Structure

### Imports

```typescript
import { CompanionActionDefinition } from '@companion-module/base'
import { YourConfig } from '../config.js'
import { InstanceBaseExt } from '../utils.js'
// Add any other helpers you need, e.g.:
// import { someHelper } from './action-utils.js'
```

> `InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>` is the typed instance — it provides access to module state, `parseVariablesInString`, `log`, `checkFeedbacks`, `saveConfig`, etc.

### Enum of Action IDs

Every action file exports an enum that names all its actions. This enum is the key type for the return object and is re-exported to the aggregator.

```typescript
export enum ActionIdMyCategory {
	doSomething = 'myDevice_doSomething',
	doSomethingElse = 'myDevice_doSomethingElse',
}
```

> Convention: enum member names are camelCase; string values are the action IDs registered with Companion (snake_case or camelCase, your choice — just be consistent).

### The Factory Function

```typescript
export function GetActionsMyCategory(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): {
	[id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
} {
	const actions: { [id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } = {
		[ActionIdMyCategory.doSomething]: {
			name: 'Do Something',
			options: [],
			callback: (): void => {
				// call a method on the instance to trigger device behavior
				instance.someDeviceMethod()
			},
		},

		[ActionIdMyCategory.doSomethingElse]: {
			name: 'Do Something Else',
			description: 'Optional help text shown in Companion UI',
			options: [
				{
					id: 'targetName',
					type: 'textinput',
					label: 'Target Name',
					default: '',
				},
			],
			callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
				// Parse Companion variables embedded in text options
				const name = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.targetName as string)
				instance.someDeviceMethodWithArg(name)
			},
		},
	}

	return actions
}
```

### Option Types — Quick Reference

| Type            | Required extra fields                   | Notes                                    |
| --------------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `textinput`     | `default: string`                       | Supports Companion variable substitution |
| `number`        | `default`, `min`, `max`                 | Add `range: true` for slider             |
| `dropdown`      | `choices: [{id, label}]`, `default`     | Single selection                         |
| `multidropdown` | `choices: [{id, label}]`, `default: []` | Multi selection                          |
| `checkbox`      | `default: boolean`                      | On/off toggle                            |
| `static-text`   | `value: string`                         | Read-only label/note                     |

**Examples:**

```typescript
// dropdown
{
  id: 'mode',
  type: 'dropdown',
  label: 'Mode',
  choices: [
    { id: 'auto', label: 'Auto' },
    { id: 'manual', label: 'Manual' },
  ],
  default: 'auto',
}

// number with slider
{
  id: 'level',
  type: 'number',
  label: 'Level',
  min: 0,
  max: 100,
  default: 50,
  range: true,
}

// checkbox
{
  id: 'enabled',
  type: 'checkbox',
  label: 'Enable Feature',
  default: false,
}
```

### Accessing Instance State in Callbacks

The `instance` parameter is the full module instance. Common patterns:

```typescript
callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
	// Read module state
	const currentValue = instance.someStateProperty

	// Parse Companion variables in string options
	const text = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.someText as string)

	// Log messages
	instance.log('info', `Doing something with ${text}`)
	instance.log('warn', 'Something unexpected')
	instance.log('error', 'Something failed')

	// Trigger feedbacks to re-evaluate
	instance.checkFeedbacks('someFeedbackId')

	// Mutate config and save
	instance.config.someFlag = true
	instance.saveConfig(instance.config)

	// Call your device integration layer
	instance.sendToDevice(text)
}
```

> Cast `action.options.*` values explicitly — TypeScript types them as `any`: `action.options.level as number`, `action.options.name as string`.

---

## Pattern 2 — The Aggregator (`actions.ts`)

`actions.ts` has three responsibilities:

1. Import every category's enum and factory function
2. Call each factory, store the typed result in a local variable
3. Build a combined object (spread all categories) and return it

### Import each file

```typescript
import { ActionIdMyCategory, GetActionsMyCategory } from './actions/action-my-category.js'
```

### Call each factory and type the local variable

```typescript
const actionsMyCategory: { [id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } =
	GetActionsMyCategory(instance)
```

### Extend the union type on the combined `actions` object

The `actions` const has a mapped type whose key is a union of **all** category enums:

```typescript
const actions: {
	[id in
		| ActionId // ← local enum for one-off actions defined inline
		| ActionIdMyCategory
		| ActionIdOtherCategory]: /* ... */ CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
} = {
	...actionsMyCategory,
	...actionsOtherCategory,
	/* ... inline one-off actions ... */
}
```

### Return and hand off to Companion

```typescript
export function GetActions(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): CompanionActionDefinitions {
	// ... (all the above) ...
	return actions
}
```

`index.ts` then calls:

```typescript
this.setActionDefinitions(GetActions(this))
```

---

## Pattern 3 — Step-by-Step Recipe

### 1. Create the file

```
src/actions/action-{category}.ts
```

Use the template below (copy verbatim, replace all `{placeholders}`).

### 2. File template

```typescript
import { CompanionActionDefinition } from '@companion-module/base'
import { YourConfig } from '../config.js'
import { InstanceBaseExt } from '../utils.js'

export enum ActionId{Category} {
  firstAction = '{category}_firstAction',
  secondAction = '{category}_secondAction',
}

export function GetActions{Category}(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): {
  [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
} {
  const actions: { [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } = {

    [ActionId{Category}.firstAction]: {
      name: 'First Action',
      options: [],
      callback: (): void => {
        instance.log('debug', 'firstAction triggered')
        // TODO: call device integration layer
      },
    },

    [ActionId{Category}.secondAction]: {
      name: 'Second Action',
      options: [
        {
          id: 'param',
          type: 'textinput',
          label: 'Parameter',
          default: '',
        },
      ],
      callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
        const param = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.param as string)
        instance.log('debug', `secondAction triggered with param: ${param}`)
        // TODO: call device integration layer with param
      },
    },

  }

  return actions
}
```

### 3. Import in `actions.ts`

Add at the top of `actions.ts` alongside the other imports:

```typescript
import { ActionId{Category}, GetActions{Category} } from './actions/action-{category}.js'
```

### 4. Call the factory in `GetActions()`

Add inside `GetActions()`, alongside the other factory calls:

```typescript
const actions{Category}: { [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } =
  GetActions{Category}(instance)
```

### 5. Extend the union type and spread into `actions`

In the `actions` const, add `ActionId{Category}` to the union type:

```typescript
const actions: {
  [id in
    | ActionId
    | /* ... existing enums ... */
    | ActionId{Category}   // ← add here
    ]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
} = {
  /* ... existing spreads ... */
  ...actions{Category},   // ← add here
}
```

### 6. Build and verify

```bash
yarn build
# or: npm run build
```

Zero TypeScript errors means your new file is properly typed and wired.

---

## Common Mistakes

| Mistake                                            | Fix                                                             |
| -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Enum string value duplicates an existing action ID | Check all other enums — IDs must be globally unique             |
| Added spread but forgot to add enum to union type  | TypeScript will error — add the enum to the `[id in ...]` union |
| Forgot `.js` extension on import in `actions.ts`   | This is ESM — always use `.js` extension on relative imports    |
| `callback` not marked `async` but uses `await`     | Add `async` keyword and change return type to `Promise<void>`   |
| Used `action.options.x` without casting            | Cast: `action.options.x as string` / `as number` / `as boolean` |

## References

- `src/actions.ts` — the aggregator (authoritative example of the full pattern)
- `src/actions/action-user-video-mic.ts` — clean example of a simple multi-action file
- `src/actions/action-social-stream.ts` — example showing non-OSC side effects (HTTP API call, config mutation)
- `src/actions/action-global.ts` — example with more complex callbacks and state access
- **companion-actions** skill — covers the `@companion-module/base` API types in depth
<exited with exit code 0>
````

</details>

/Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso/.squad/skills/make-skill-template/SKILL.md

<details>
<summary>148 lines</summary>

````
1. ---
2. name: make-skill-template
3. description: 'Create new Agent Skills for GitHub Copilot from prompts or by duplicating this template. Use when asked to "create a skill", "make a new skill", "scaffold a skill", or when building specialized AI capabilities with bundled resources. Generates SKILL.md files with proper frontmatter, directory structure, and optional scripts/references/assets folders.'
4. ---
5.
6. # Make Skill Template
7.
8. A meta-skill for creating new Agent Skills. Use this skill when you need to scaffold a new skill folder, generate a SKILL.md file, or help users understand the Agent Skills specification.
9.
10. ## When to Use This Skill
11.
12. - User asks to "create a skill", "make a new skill", or "scaffold a skill"
13. - User wants to add a specialized capability to their GitHub Copilot setup
14. - User needs help structuring a skill with bundled resources
15. - User wants to duplicate this template as a starting point
16.
17. ## Prerequisites
18.
19. - Understanding of what the skill should accomplish
20. - A clear, keyword-rich description of capabilities and triggers
21. - Knowledge of any bundled resources needed (scripts, references, assets, templates)
22.
23. ## Creating a New Skill
24.
25. ### Step 1: Create the Skill Directory
26.
27. Create a new folder with a lowercase, hyphenated name:
28.
29. ```
30. skills/<skill-name>/
31. └── SKILL.md          # Required
32. ```
33.
34. ### Step 2: Generate SKILL.md with Frontmatter
35.
36. Every skill requires YAML frontmatter with `name` and `description`:
37.
38. ```yaml
39. ---
40. name: <skill-name>
41. description: '<What it does>. Use when <specific triggers, scenarios, keywords users might say>.'
42. ---
43. ```
44.
45. #### Frontmatter Field Requirements
46.
47. | Field           | Required | Constraints                                                                |
48. | --------------- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
49. | `name`          | **Yes**  | 1-64 chars, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens only, must match folder name |
50. | `description`   | **Yes**  | 1-1024 chars, must describe WHAT it does AND WHEN to use it                |
51. | `license`       | No       | License name or reference to bundled LICENSE.txt                           |
52. | `compatibility` | No       | 1-500 chars, environment requirements if needed                            |
53. | `metadata`      | No       | Key-value pairs for additional properties                                  |
54. | `allowed-tools` | No       | Space-delimited list of pre-approved tools (experimental)                  |
55.
56. #### Description Best Practices
57.
58. **CRITICAL**: The `description` is the PRIMARY mechanism for automatic skill discovery. Include:
59.
60. 1. **WHAT** the skill does (capabilities)
61. 2. **WHEN** to use it (triggers, scenarios, file types)
62. 3. **Keywords** users might mention in prompts
63.
64. **Good example:**
65.
66. ```yaml
67. description: 'Toolkit for testing local web applications using Playwright. Use when asked to verify frontend functionality, debug UI behavior, capture browser screenshots, or view browser console logs. Supports Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit.'
68. ```
69.
70. **Poor example:**
71.
72. ```yaml
73. description: 'Web testing helpers'
74. ```
75.
76. ### Step 3: Write the Skill Body
77.
78. After the frontmatter, add markdown instructions. Recommended sections:
79.
80. | Section                     | Purpose                         |
81. | --------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
82. | `# Title`                   | Brief overview                  |
83. | `## When to Use This Skill` | Reinforces description triggers |
84. | `## Prerequisites`          | Required tools, dependencies    |
85. | `## Step-by-Step Workflows` | Numbered steps for tasks        |
86. | `## Troubleshooting`        | Common issues and solutions     |
87. | `## References`             | Links to bundled docs           |
88.
89. ### Step 4: Add Optional Directories (If Needed)
90.
91. | Folder        | Purpose                            | When to Use                         |
92. | ------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
93. | `scripts/`    | Executable code (Python, Bash, JS) | Automation that performs operations |
94. | `references/` | Documentation agent reads          | API references, schemas, guides     |
95. | `assets/`     | Static files used AS-IS            | Images, fonts, templates            |
96. | `templates/`  | Starter code agent modifies        | Scaffolds to extend                 |
97.
98. ## Example: Complete Skill Structure
99.
100. ```
101. my-awesome-skill/
102. ├── SKILL.md                    # Required instructions
103. ├── LICENSE.txt                 # Optional license file
104. ├── scripts/
105. │   └── helper.py               # Executable automation
106. ├── references/
107. │   ├── api-reference.md        # Detailed docs
108. │   └── examples.md             # Usage examples
109. ├── assets/
110. │   └── diagram.png             # Static resources
111. └── templates/
112.     └── starter.ts              # Code scaffold
113. ```
114.
115. ## Quick Start: Duplicate This Template
116.
117. 1. Copy the `make-skill-template/` folder
118. 2. Rename to your skill name (lowercase, hyphens)
119. 3. Update `SKILL.md`:
120.    - Change `name:` to match folder name
121.    - Write a keyword-rich `description:`
122.    - Replace body content with your instructions
123. 4. Add bundled resources as needed
124. 5. Validate with `npm run skill:validate`
125.
126. ## Validation Checklist
127.
128. - [ ] Folder name is lowercase with hyphens
129. - [ ] `name` field matches folder name exactly
130. - [ ] `description` is 10-1024 characters
131. - [ ] `description` explains WHAT and WHEN
132. - [ ] `description` is wrapped in single quotes
133. - [ ] Body content is under 500 lines
134. - [ ] Bundled assets are under 5MB each
135.
136. ## Troubleshooting
137.
138. | Issue                    | Solution                                                 |
139. | ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
140. | Skill not discovered     | Improve description with more keywords and triggers      |
141. | Validation fails on name | Ensure lowercase, no consecutive hyphens, matches folder |
142. | Description too short    | Add capabilities, triggers, and keywords                 |
143. | Assets not found         | Use relative paths from skill root                       |
144.
145. ## References
146.
147. - Agent Skills official spec: <https://agentskills.io/specification>
148.
````

</details>

/Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso/src/actions/action-global-recording.ts

<details>
<summary>153 lines</summary>

```
1. import { CompanionActionDefinition } from '@companion-module/base'
2. import { ZoomConfig } from '../config.js'
3. import { InstanceBaseExt } from '../utils.js'
4. import { createCommand, sendActionCommand } from './action-utils.js'
5.
6. export enum ActionIdGlobalRecording {
7. 	startLocalRecording = 'startLocalRecording',
8. 	pauseLocalRecording = 'pauseLocalRecording',
9. 	resumeLocalRecording = 'resumeLocalRecording',
10. 	stopLocalRecording = 'stopLocalRecording',
11. 	startCloudRecording = 'startCloudRecording',
12. 	pauseCloudRecording = 'pauseCloudRecording',
13. 	resumeCloudRecording = 'resumeCloudRecording',
14. 	stopCloudRecording = 'stopCloudRecording',
15. }
16.
17. export function GetActionsGlobalRecording(instance: InstanceBaseExt<ZoomConfig>): {
18. 	[id in ActionIdGlobalRecording]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
19. } {
20. 	const actions: { [id in ActionIdGlobalRecording]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } = {
21. 		[ActionIdGlobalRecording.startLocalRecording]: {
22. 			name: 'Start Local Recording',
23. 			options: [],
24. 			callback: (): void => {
25. 				// type: 'Global'
26. 				const command = createCommand(instance, '/startLocalRecording')
27. 				const sendToCommand = {
28. 					id: ActionIdGlobalRecording.startLocalRecording,
29. 					options: {
30. 						command: command.oscPath,
31. 						args: command.args,
32. 					},
33. 				}
34. 				sendActionCommand(instance, sendToCommand)
35. 			},
36. 		},
37. 		[ActionIdGlobalRecording.pauseLocalRecording]: {
38. 			name: 'Pause Local Recording',
39. 			options: [],
40. 			callback: (): void => {
41. 				// type: 'Global'
42. 				const command = createCommand(instance, '/pauseLocalRecording')
43. 				const sendToCommand = {
44. 					id: ActionIdGlobalRecording.pauseLocalRecording,
45. 					options: {
46. 						command: command.oscPath,
47. 						args: command.args,
48. 					},
49. 				}
50. 				sendActionCommand(instance, sendToCommand)
51. 			},
52. 		},
53. 		[ActionIdGlobalRecording.resumeLocalRecording]: {
54. 			name: 'Resume Local Recording',
55. 			options: [],

59. 				const sendToCommand = {
60. 					id: ActionIdGlobalRecording.resumeLocalRecording,
61. 					options: {
62. 						command: command.oscPath,
63. 						args: command.args,
64. 					},
65. 				}
66. 				sendActionCommand(instance, sendToCommand)
67. 			},

70. 			name: 'Stop Local Recording',

<sub>⏱️ 9892m 40s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

/Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso/.squad/skills/companion-action-file-pattern/SKILL.md

<details>
<summary>368 lines</summary>

````
1. ---
2. name: companion-action-file-pattern
3. description: 'Teaches the multi-file action pattern used in split-file Companion modules. Use when asked to add a new action category, create an action file, register actions in an aggregator, or extend the actions layer of a Companion module that splits actions across multiple files with a GetActions aggregator.'
4. ---
5.
6. # Companion Action File Pattern
7.
8. This module splits action definitions across many files (one per category), then aggregates them in a single `actions.ts` that calls `setActionDefinitions()`. This skill documents the exact structure and wiring required to add a new action category.
9.
10. ## When to Use This Skill
11.
12. ### ✅ Use this skill when:
13. - You are adding a **new logical category** of actions that does not fit into any existing `src/actions/action-*.ts` file
14. - You need to create a brand new `src/actions/action-{category}.ts` file from scratch
15. - You need to wire a new file into the `actions.ts` aggregator for the first time
16. - The new actions are conceptually distinct from existing categories (e.g., adding breakout room actions when none exist, or adding a recording actions file)
17.
18. ### ❌ Do NOT use this skill when:
19. - You only need to **add one or more actions to an existing file** — just open the existing `src/actions/action-{category}.ts`, add the new enum member(s) and action definition(s), and you're done. No new file, no aggregator changes needed.
20. - You are modifying or renaming an existing action definition
21. - You are adding a single utility action that logically belongs to a category that already has a file
22.
23. **The rule of thumb:** If a file for your category already exists → edit it directly. If no file exists for your category → use this skill to create one and wire it up.
24.
25. ## Pattern Overview
26.
27. ```
28. src/
29.   actions.ts                        ← aggregator (imports + combines all categories)
30.   actions/
31.     action-{category-a}.ts          ← one file per action category
32.     action-{category-b}.ts
33.     action-{category-c}.ts
34.     action-utils.ts                 ← shared helpers used by action files
35. ```
36.
37. `index.ts` calls:
38.
39. ```typescript
40. this.setActionDefinitions(GetActions(this))
41. ```
42.
43. `GetActions()` (in `actions.ts`) calls each category's `GetActions{Category}(instance)`, collects the typed objects, spreads them into one combined object, and returns it.
44.
45. ---
46.
47. ## Pattern 1 — The Action File Structure
48.
49. ### Imports
50.
51. ```typescript
52. import { CompanionActionDefinition } from '@companion-module/base'
53. import { YourConfig } from '../config.js'
54. import { InstanceBaseExt } from '../utils.js'
55. // Add any other helpers you need, e.g.:
56. // import { someHelper } from './action-utils.js'
57. ```
58.
59. > `InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>` is the typed instance — it provides access to module state, `parseVariablesInString`, `log`, `checkFeedbacks`, `saveConfig`, etc.
60.
61. ### Enum of Action IDs
62.
63. Every action file exports an enum that names all its actions. This enum is the key type for the return object and is re-exported to the aggregator.
64.
65. ```typescript
66. export enum ActionIdMyCategory {
67. 	doSomething = 'myDevice_doSomething',
68. 	doSomethingElse = 'myDevice_doSomethingElse',
69. }
70. ```
71.
72. > Convention: enum member names are camelCase; string values are the action IDs registered with Companion (snake_case or camelCase, your choice — just be consistent).
73.
74. ### The Factory Function
75.
76. ```typescript
77. export function GetActionsMyCategory(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): {
78. 	[id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
79. } {
80. 	const actions: { [id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } = {
81. 		[ActionIdMyCategory.doSomething]: {
82. 			name: 'Do Something',
83. 			options: [],
84. 			callback: (): void => {
85. 				// call a method on the instance to trigger device behavior
86. 				instance.someDeviceMethod()
87. 			},
88. 		},
89.
90. 		[ActionIdMyCategory.doSomethingElse]: {
91. 			name: 'Do Something Else',
92. 			description: 'Optional help text shown in Companion UI',
93. 			options: [
94. 				{
95. 					id: 'targetName',
96. 					type: 'textinput',
97. 					label: 'Target Name',
98. 					default: '',
99. 				},
100. 			],
101. 			callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
102. 				// Parse Companion variables embedded in text options
103. 				const name = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.targetName as string)
104. 				instance.someDeviceMethodWithArg(name)
105. 			},
106. 		},
107. 	}
108.
109. 	return actions
110. }
111. ```
112.
113. ### Option Types — Quick Reference
114.
115. | Type            | Required extra fields                   | Notes                                    |
116. | --------------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
117. | `textinput`     | `default: string`                       | Supports Companion variable substitution |
118. | `number`        | `default`, `min`, `max`                 | Add `range: true` for slider             |
119. | `dropdown`      | `choices: [{id, label}]`, `default`     | Single selection                         |
120. | `multidropdown` | `choices: [{id, label}]`, `default: []` | Multi selection                          |
121. | `checkbox`      | `default: boolean`                      | On/off toggle                            |
122. | `static-text`   | `value: string`                         | Read-only label/note                     |
123.
124. **Examples:**
125.
126. ```typescript
127. // dropdown
128. {
129.   id: 'mode',
130.   type: 'dropdown',
131.   label: 'Mode',
132.   choices: [
133.     { id: 'auto', label: 'Auto' },
134.     { id: 'manual', label: 'Manual' },
135.   ],
136.   default: 'auto',
137. }
138.
139. // number with slider
140. {
141.   id: 'level',
142.   type: 'number',
143.   label: 'Level',
144.   min: 0,
145.   max: 100,
146.   default: 50,
147.   range: true,
148. }
149.
150. // checkbox
151. {
152.   id: 'enabled',
153.   type: 'checkbox',
154.   label: 'Enable Feature',
155.   default: false,
156. }
157. ```
158.
159. ### Accessing Instance State in Callbacks
160.
161. The `instance` parameter is the full module instance. Common patterns:
162.
163. ```typescript
164. callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
165. 	// Read module state
166. 	const currentValue = instance.someStateProperty
167.
168. 	// Parse Companion variables in string options
169. 	const text = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.someText as string)
170.
171. 	// Log messages
172. 	instance.log('info', `Doing something with ${text}`)
173. 	instance.log('warn', 'Something unexpected')
174. 	instance.log('error', 'Something failed')
175.
176. 	// Trigger feedbacks to re-evaluate
177. 	instance.checkFeedbacks('someFeedbackId')
178.
179. 	// Mutate config and save
180. 	instance.config.someFlag = true
181. 	instance.saveConfig(instance.config)
182.
183. 	// Call your device integration layer
184. 	instance.sendToDevice(text)
185. }
186. ```
187.
188. > Cast `action.options.*` values explicitly — TypeScript types them as `any`: `action.options.level as number`, `action.options.name as string`.
189.
190. ---
191.
192. ## Pattern 2 — The Aggregator (`actions.ts`)
193.
194. `actions.ts` has three responsibilities:
195.
196. 1. Import every category's enum and factory function
197. 2. Call each factory, store the typed result in a local variable
198. 3. Build a combined object (spread all categories) and return it
199.
200. ### Import each file
201.
202. ```typescript
203. import { ActionIdMyCategory, GetActionsMyCategory } from './actions/action-my-category.js'
204. ```
205.
206. ### Call each factory and type the local variable
207.
208. ```typescript
209. const actionsMyCategory: { [id in ActionIdMyCategory]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } =
210. 	GetActionsMyCategory(instance)
211. ```
212.
213. ### Extend the union type on the combined `actions` object
214.
215. The `actions` const has a mapped type whose key is a union of **all** category enums:
216.
217. ```typescript
218. const actions: {
219. 	[id in
220. 		| ActionId // ← local enum for one-off actions defined inline
221. 		| ActionIdMyCategory
222. 		| ActionIdOtherCategory]: /* ... */ CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
223. } = {
224. 	...actionsMyCategory,
225. 	...actionsOtherCategory,
226. 	/* ... inline one-off actions ... */
227. }
228. ```
229.
230. ### Return and hand off to Companion
231.
232. ```typescript
233. export function GetActions(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): CompanionActionDefinitions {
234. 	// ... (all the above) ...
235. 	return actions
236. }
237. ```
238.
239. `index.ts` then calls:
240.
241. ```typescript
242. this.setActionDefinitions(GetActions(this))
243. ```
244.
245. ---
246.
247. ## Pattern 3 — Step-by-Step Recipe
248.
249. ### 1. Create the file
250.
251. ```
252. src/actions/action-{category}.ts
253. ```
254.
255. Use the template below (copy verbatim, replace all `{placeholders}`).
256.
257. ### 2. File template
258.
259. ```typescript
260. import { CompanionActionDefinition } from '@companion-module/base'
261. import { YourConfig } from '../config.js'
262. import { InstanceBaseExt } from '../utils.js'
263.
264. export enum ActionId{Category} {
265.   firstAction = '{category}_firstAction',
266.   secondAction = '{category}_secondAction',
267. }
268.
269. export function GetActions{Category}(instance: InstanceBaseExt<YourConfig>): {
270.   [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
271. } {
272.   const actions: { [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } = {
273.
274.     [ActionId{Category}.firstAction]: {
275.       name: 'First Action',
276.       options: [],
277.       callback: (): void => {
278.         instance.log('debug', 'firstAction triggered')
279.         // TODO: call device integration layer
280.       },
281.     },
282.
283.     [ActionId{Category}.secondAction]: {
284.       name: 'Second Action',
285.       options: [
286.         {
287.           id: 'param',
288.           type: 'textinput',
289.           label: 'Parameter',
290.           default: '',
291.         },
292.       ],
293.       callback: async (action): Promise<void> => {
294.         const param = await instance.parseVariablesInString(action.options.param as string)
295.         instance.log('debug', `secondAction triggered with param: ${param}`)
296.         // TODO: call device integration layer with param
297.       },
298.     },
299.
300.   }
301.
302.   return actions
303. }
304. ```
305.
306. ### 3. Import in `actions.ts`
307.
308. Add at the top of `actions.ts` alongside the other imports:
309.
310. ```typescript
311. import { ActionId{Category}, GetActions{Category} } from './actions/action-{category}.js'
312. ```
313.
314. ### 4. Call the factory in `GetActions()`
315.
316. Add inside `GetActions()`, alongside the other factory calls:
317.
318. ```typescript
319. const actions{Category}: { [id in ActionId{Category}]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined } =
320.   GetActions{Category}(instance)
321. ```
322.
323. ### 5. Extend the union type and spread into `actions`
324.
325. In the `actions` const, add `ActionId{Category}` to the union type:
326.
327. ```typescript
328. const actions: {
329.   [id in
330.     | ActionId
331.     | /* ... existing enums ... */
332.     | ActionId{Category}   // ← add here
333.     ]: CompanionActionDefinition | undefined
334. } = {
335.   /* ... existing spreads ... */
336.   ...actions{Category},   // ← add here
337. }
338. ```
339.
340. ### 6. Build and verify
341.
342. ```bash
343. yarn build
344. # or: npm run build
345. ```
346.
347. Zero TypeScript errors means your new file is properly typed and wired.
348.
349. ---
350.
351. ## Common Mistakes
352.
353. | Mistake                                            | Fix                                                             |
354. | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
355. | Enum string value duplicates an existing action ID | Check all other enums — IDs must be globally unique             |
356. | Added spread but forgot to add enum to union type  | TypeScript will error — add the enum to the `[id in ...]` union |
357. | Forgot `.js` extension on import in `actions.ts`   | This is ESM — always use `.js` extension on relative imports    |
358. | `callback` not marked `async` but uses `await`     | Add `async` keyword and change return type to `Promise<void>`   |
359. | Used `action.options.x` without casting            | Cast: `action.options.x as string` / `as number` / `as boolean` |
360.
361. ## References
362.
363. - `src/actions.ts` — the aggregator (authoritative example of the full pattern)
364. - `src/actions/action-user-video-mic.ts` — clean example of a simple multi-action file
365. - `src/actions/action-social-stream.ts` — example showing non-OSC side effects (HTTP API call, config mutation)
366. - `src/actions/action-global.ts` — example with more complex callbacks and state access
367. - **companion-actions** skill — covers the `@companion-module/base` API types in depth
368.
````

</details>

6. import { GetPresetsListGallery } from './presets/preset-gallery.js'

11. import { GetPresetsZoomISOSelections } from './presets/preset-zoomiso-selections.js'
12. import { GetPresetsZoomISOActions } from './presets/preset-zoomiso-actions.js'
13. import { GetPresetsReactionName } from './presets/preset-reaction-name.js'
14. import { GetPresetsRoleManagement } from './presets/preset-role-management.js'
15. import { GetPresetsJoinLeaveEnd } from './presets/preset-join-leave-end.js'
16. import { GetPresetsDeviceSettings } from './presets/preset-devices-settings.js'
17. import { GetPresetsChat } from './presets/preset-chat.js'
18. import { GetPresetsSharing } from './presets/preset-sharing.js'
19. import { GetPresetsBreakout } from './presets/preset-breakout.js'
20. import { GetPresetsRecording } from './presets/preset-recording.js'
21. import { GetPresetsDataCustom } from './presets/preset-data-custom.js'
22. import { ZoomConfig } from './config.js'
23.
24. export function GetPresetList(instance: InstanceBaseExt<ZoomConfig>): CompanionPresetDefinitions {
25. 	const presetsParticipants = GetPresetsListParticipants(instance)
26. 	const presetsGallery = GetPresetsListGallery(instance)
27. 	const presetsManageSelections = GetPresetsManageSelections()
28. 	const presetsGroups = GetPresetsGroups(instance)
29. 	const presetsPinSpotlightViewActions = GetPresetsPinSpotlightViewActions()
30. 	const presetsVideoAudioActions = GetPresetsVideoAudioActions()
31. 	const presetsZoomISOSelections = GetPresetsZoomISOSelections(instance)
32. 	const presetsZoomISOActions = GetPresetsZoomISOActions(instance)
33. 	const presetsReactionNames = GetPresetsReactionName(instance)
34. 	const presetsRoleManagement = GetPresetsRoleManagement()
35. 	const presetsJoinLeaveEnd = GetPresetsJoinLeaveEnd()
36. 	const presetsDeviceSettings = GetPresetsDeviceSettings()
37. 	const presetChat = GetPresetsChat()
38. 	const presetSharing = GetPresetsSharing()
39. 	const presetBreakout = GetPresetsBreakout()
40. 	const presetRecording = GetPresetsRecording()
41. 	const presetDataCustom = GetPresetsDataCustom()
42.
43. 	const presets: CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt = {
44. 		...presetsParticipants,
45. 		...presetsGallery,
46. 		...presetsManageSelections,
47. 		...presetsGroups,
48. 		...presetsPinSpotlightViewActions,
49. 		...presetsVideoAudioActions,
50. 		...presetsZoomISOSelections,
51. 		...presetsZoomISOActions,
52. 		...presetsReactionNames,
53. 		...presetsRoleManagement,
54. 		...presetsJoinLeaveEnd,
55. 		...presetsDeviceSettings,
56. 		...presetChat,
57. 		...presetSharing,
58. 		...presetBreakout,
59. 		...presetRecording,
60. 		...presetDataCustom,
61. 	}
62.
63. 	return presets
64. }
65.
```

68. 					| ActionIdUserWebinar
69. 					| ActionIdZoomISOEngine
70. 					| ActionIdZoomISOOutputSettings
71. 					| ActionIdZoomISORouting
72. 					| ActionIdZoomISOActions
73. 					| ActionIdUsers
74. 			} & CompanionButtonPresetDefinition['steps'][0]['down'][0]
75. 		>
76. 		up: Array<
77. 			{
78. 				actionId:
79. 					| ActionId
80. 					| ActionIdGroups
81. 					| ActionIdGallery
82. 					| ActionIdGlobalBreakoutRooms
83. 					| ActionIdGlobalMemoryManagement
84. 					| ActionIdGlobalGalleryTrackingAndDataRequest
85. 					| ActionIdGlobalRecording
86. 					| ActionIdGlobalWaitingRoomsAndZak
87. 					| ActionIdGlobal
88. 					| ActionIdUserBreakoutRooms
89. 					| ActionIdUserChat
90. 					| ActionIdUserHandRaised
91. 					| ActionIdUserPin
92. 					| ActionIdUserRolesAndAction
93. 					| ActionIdUserScreenshare
94. 					| ActionIdUserSettings
95. 					| ActionIdUserSpotlight
96. 					| ActionIdUserVideoMic
97. 					| ActionIdUserView
98. 					| ActionIdUserWaitingRoom
99. 					| ActionIdUserWebinar
100. 					| ActionIdZoomISOEngine
101. 					| ActionIdZoomISOOutputSettings
102. 					| ActionIdZoomISORouting
103. 					| ActionIdZoomISOActions
104. 					| ActionIdUsers
105. 			} & CompanionButtonPresetDefinition['steps'][0]['up'][0]
106. 		>
107. 	}>
108. }
109. export interface CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt {
110. 	[id: string]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined
111. }
112.
113. export const buttonTextDefaultLength = 50
114. export const buttonTextActiveSpeakerLength = 40
115. export const alignmentTopLeft = 'left:top'
116. export const alignmentTopCenter = 'center:top'
117.
118. export const getFeedbackStyleSelected = (): CompanionFeedbackButtonStyleResult => {
119. 	return {
120. 		color: colorBlack,
121. 		bgcolor: colorDarkGray,
122. 	}
123. }
124.
125. export const getFeedbackStyleSpotlight = (): CompanionFeedbackButtonStyleResult => {
126. 	return {
127. 		color: colorWhite,
128. 		bgcolor: colorDarkRed,
129. 	}
130. }
131.
132. export const getParticipantStyleDefault = (
133. 	instance: InstanceBaseExt<ZoomConfig>,
134. 	text: string,
135. 	position: number,
136. ): any => {
137. 	return {
138. 		text:
139. 			instance.config.feedbackImagesWithIcons === 4
140. 				? `${position}. ${text}`
141. 				: `\`${position}. \${substr(${text},0,${buttonTextDefaultLength})}\``,
142. 		size: '10',
143. 		color: colorWhite,
144. 		bgcolor: colorBlack,
145. 		alignment: alignmentTopCenter,
146. 		show_topbar: false,
147. 		textExpression: instance.config.feedbackImagesWithIcons === 4 ? false : true,
148. 	} //as CompanionButtonStyleProps
149. }
150.
151. export const getParticipantStyleActiveSpeaker = (text: string, position: number): CompanionButtonStyleProps => {
152. 	return {
153. 		text: `\`\\n${position}. \${substr(${text},0,${buttonTextActiveSpeakerLength})}\``,
154. 		alignment: alignmentTopLeft,
155. 	} as CompanionButtonStyleProps
156. }
157.
```

</details>

{
	"agent_type": "general-purpose",

	"mode": "background",
	"prompt": "\nYou are Merry, the Module Dev on this project.\nTEAM ROOT: /Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso\n**Requested by:** Justin James\n\nTASK: Create `.squad/skills/companion-preset-category-file/SKILL.md`\n\nThis skill teaches how to **create a new preset category file and wire it into the presets.ts aggregator** — the equivalent of `companion-action-file-pattern` but for presets.\n\n## Reference files to read first\n- `.squad/skills/make-skill-template/SKILL.md` — valid frontmatter spec\n- `.squad/skills/companion-action-file-pattern/SKILL.md` — the parallel action skill to mirror in structure\n- `src/presets.ts` — the aggregator\n- `src/presets/preset-recording.ts` — a clean, small preset category file example\n- `src/presets/preset-utils.ts` — the shared types used across all preset files\n\n## Key differences from the action pattern (CRITICAL — get these right)\n\n1. **No enum** — presets use plain string keys (`presets['My_Preset_Key'] = { ... }`) not enum members\n2. **Return type** — `CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt` (from `./preset-utils.js`), NOT a TypeScript mapped type\n3. **No union type extension** — adding a new category to `presets.ts` does NOT require adding to a `[id in ...]` union — just import, call, spread\n4. **Factory signature** — some factories take `instance`, some don't (depends on whether the preset needs dynamic data like participant lists). Document both forms.\n5. **CompanionPresetExt structure** — each preset entry has: `type: 'button'`, `category`, `name`, `style` (with `text`, `size`, `color`, `bgcolor`), `steps` (array of `{ down: [...], up: [] }`), `feedbacks`\n6. **actionId in steps** — use the appropriate `ActionId*` enum value imported from the relevant action file. The `actionId` field is typed to the union in `CompanionPresetExt` (from preset-utils.ts)\n\n## Skill metadata (valid fields only: name, description)\n\n- `name: companion-preset-category-file`\n- `description`: single-quoted, 10-1024 chars, WHAT + WHEN + keywords including \"preset category file\", \"create preset file\", \"add preset category\", \"wire presets\", \"presets.ts aggregator\"\n\n## Skill body must include\n\n1. **`## When to Use This Skill`** — explicit ✅/❌:\n   - ✅ Use when: creating a brand new `src/presets/preset-{category}.ts` file\n   - ✅ Use when: wiring a new preset category into `presets.ts` for the first time\n   - ❌ NOT when: adding a preset to an existing category file (→ use `companion-add-preset-to-category-file`)\n\n2. **`## Pattern Overview`** — directory structure, how `presets.ts` aggregates\n\n3. **`## Pattern 1 — The Preset Category File`** — show the file structure:\n   - Imports: `CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt` from `./preset-utils.js`, action enum(s) from action files, any helpers\n   - Factory function: both variants (with and without `instance`)\n   - How to add a preset entry (plain string key, `CompanionPresetExt` shape)\n   - A minimal button template showing all required fields\n\n4. **`## Pattern 2 — The Aggregator (presets.ts)`** — the 3 changes needed:\n   - Add import\n   - Call factory and store result\n   - Spread into combined `presets` object\n\n5. **`## Step-by-Step Recipe`** — 5 numbered steps (create file, write factory, add presets, import in presets.ts, wire into aggregator)\n\n6. **`## Preset Button Shape`** — a reference table or annotated example showing all fields of a `CompanionPresetExt`:\n\n```typescript\npresets['My_Preset_Key'] = {\n  type: 'button',\n  category: 'My Category',       // groups presets in Companion UI\n  name: 'My_Preset_Key',         // display name\n  style: {\n    text: 'Button Label',\n    size: '14',\n    color: colorBlack,           // from '../utils.js'\n    bgcolor: colorLightGray,     // from '../utils.js'\n  },\n  steps: [\n    {\n      down: [\n        {\n          actionId: ActionIdMyCategory.someAction,  // typed enum value\n          options: {},\n        },\n      ],\n      up: [],\n    },\n  ],\n  feedbacks: [],\n}\n```\n\n7. **`## Common Mistakes`** — short table:\n   - Using a string literal for `actionId` instead of an enum value → TypeScript will not catch typos\n   - Forgetting `.js` extension on imports\n   - Not spreading the result into the combined `presets` object in `presets.ts`\n   - Using `any` for `actionId` — defeats the type safety; use the correct `ActionId*` enum\n\n8. **`## References`** — link to the sibling skill, `src/presets.ts`, and `src/presets/preset-recording.ts`\n\n## Tone and length\nUnder 250 lines. Focused. A developer should be able to follow it without reading another file.\n\n## Output location\nCreate: `.squad/skills/companion-preset-category-file/SKILL.md`\n\n## After writing\nWrite to `.squad/decisions/inbox/merry-preset-category-file-skill.md`:\n```\n### 2026-03-01: New skill — companion-preset-category-file\n**By:** Merry (requested by Justin James)\n**What:** Created skill for adding a new preset category file + wiring into presets.ts aggregator.\n**Why:** Complements companion-action-file-pattern for the presets layer; enables consistent multi-file preset architecture.\n```\n\n⚠️ OUTPUT: Confirm what was created and that frontmatter is valid (name matches folder, no extra fields).\n⚠️ RESPONSE ORDER: After ALL tool calls, write a plain text summary as FINAL output. No tool calls after this summary.\n"
}

	"prompt": "\nYou are Merry, the Module Dev on this project.\nTEAM ROOT: /Users/lynbh/Development/companion-module-dev/companion-module-zoom-osc-iso\n**Requested by:** Justin James\n\nTASK: Completely rewrite `.squad/skills/companion-preset-category-file/SKILL.md` to use a **TypeScript enum pattern** for preset IDs — matching how the action files use enums. The current version uses plain string keys (`presets['My_Key'] = { ... }`). The user wants the new target architecture to use enums instead.\n\n## Read these files first\n- `.squad/skills/companion-action-file-pattern/SKILL.md` — the EXACT model to mirror. The preset skill should be structurally identical but adapted for presets.\n- `src/actions.ts` — how the action aggregator uses union type + spread\n- `src/presets.ts` — the current preset aggregator (will need the same union type treatment)\n- `src/presets/preset-utils.ts` — `CompanionPresetExt` and `CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt` types\n\n## The new pattern (enum-based presets)\n\n### The category file pattern\n\n```typescript\nimport { CompanionPresetExt, CompanionPresetDefinitionsExt } from './preset-utils.js'\nimport { ActionIdSomeCategory } from '../actions/action-some-category.js'\nimport { colorBlack, colorLightGray } from '../utils.js'\n\nexport enum PresetId{Category} {\n  myPreset = '{category}_myPreset',\n  anotherPreset = '{category}_anotherPreset',\n}\n\nexport function GetPresets{Category}(): {\n  [id in PresetId{Category}]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined\n} {\n  const presets: { [id in PresetId{Category}]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined } = {\n\n    [PresetId{Category}.myPreset]: {\n      type: 'button',\n      category: 'My Category',\n      name: 'My Preset',\n      style: {\n        text: 'Button Label',\n        size: '14',\n        color: colorBlack,\n        bgcolor: colorLightGray,\n      },\n      steps: [\n        {\n          down: [\n            {\n              actionId: ActionIdSomeCategory.doSomething,\n              options: {},\n            },\n          ],\n          up: [],\n        },\n      ],\n      feedbacks: [],\n    },\n\n  }\n\n  return presets\n}\n```\n\nNote: some factories need `instance` (e.g. `GetPresetsListParticipants(instance)`) — document the `instance` variant too.\n\n### The aggregator (`presets.ts`) pattern\n\nMirrors `actions.ts` exactly:\n\n```typescript\nimport { PresetId{Category}, GetPresets{Category} } from './presets/preset-{category}.js'\n\n// inside GetPresetList():\nconst presets{Category}: { [id in PresetId{Category}]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined } =\n  GetPresets{Category}()\n\nconst presets: {\n  [id in\n    | PresetId{Category1}\n    | PresetId{Category2}   // ← add new enum here\n    ]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined\n} = {\n  ...presets{Category1},\n  ...presets{Category2},   // ← add new spread here\n}\n\nreturn presets as CompanionPresetDefinitions\n```\n\n## Key points to cover\n\n1. **Why enum over string keys**: TypeScript catches duplicate IDs, rename-refactoring works, aggregator union type enforces exhaustive coverage\n2. **Enum string values**: convention is `'{category}_{presetName}'` to namespace and avoid collisions\n3. **Union type extension** in `presets.ts` is REQUIRED — this is different from the old pattern\n4. **`as CompanionPresetDefinitions`** cast on return may be needed since the mapped type is more specific\n\n## Skill metadata (keep valid fields only)\n- `name: companion-preset-category-file` (unchanged — must match folder name)\n- `description:` — rewrite to include keywords: \"enum\", \"preset category file\", \"create preset file\", \"add preset category\", \"wire presets\", \"presets.ts aggregator\", \"enum-based preset IDs\"\n\n## Skill must include (parallel to companion-action-file-pattern)\n1. When to Use — ✅/❌ structure\n2. Pattern Overview — directory structure\n3. Pattern 1 — The Preset Category File (with enum, typed return, both instance/no-instance forms)\n4. Pattern 2 — The Aggregator (presets.ts) — same 3-change structure as actions\n5. Step-by-Step Recipe — 5-6 numbered steps\n6. Option Types / Preset Button Shape — annotated example\n7. Common Mistakes — table\n8. References\n\n## IMPORTANT: Overwrite the existing file completely\nThe file already exists at `.squad/skills/companion-preset-category-file/SKILL.md`. Replace its entire content.\n\n⚠️ OUTPUT: Confirm file was rewritten with enum pattern, valid frontmatter.\n⚠️ RESPONSE ORDER: After ALL tool calls, write a plain text summary as FINAL output. No tool calls after this summary.\n"
}

The preset skill should be structurally identical but adapted for presets.\n- `src/presets/preset-recording.ts` — current preset file (shows the string-key pattern to contrast against)\n- `src/presets/preset-utils.ts` — `CompanionPresetExt` type\n\n## The new pattern\n\nAdding a preset to an existing **enum-based** category file is exactly 2 steps:\n\n**Step 1: Add the enum member**\n```typescript\nexport enum PresetId{Category} {\n  existingPreset = '{category}_existingPreset',\n  myNewPreset = '{category}_myNewPreset',    // ← add here\n}\n```\n\n**Step 2: Add the preset definition in the `actions` object**\n```typescript\n[PresetId{Category}.myNewPreset]: {\n  type: 'button',\n  category: 'My Category',\n  name: 'My New Preset',\n  style: {\n    text: 'New Preset',\n    size: '14',\n    color: colorBlack,\n    bgcolor: colorLightGray,\n  },\n  steps: [\n    {\n      down: [\n        {\n          actionId: ActionIdSomeCategory.someAction,\n          options: {},\n        },\n      ],\n      up: [],\n    },\n  ],\n  feedbacks: [],\n},\n```\n\nNothing else changes — no `presets.ts` aggregator edits, no union type changes.\n\n## Skill metadata (valid fields only)\n- `name: companion-add-preset-to-category-file` (unchanged — must match folder name)\n- `description:` — rewrite to mention \"enum\", \"add preset\", \"existing preset category file\", \"extend preset enum\", \"preset-{category}.ts\"\n\n## Skill must include (parallel to companion-add-action-to-category-file)\n1. **When to Use** — ✅/❌:\n   - ✅ Adding preset(s) to an existing enum-based `src/presets/preset-{category}.ts`\n   - ❌ No file yet → use `companion-preset-category-file`\n   - ❌ Modifying/deleting existing presets\n\n2. **The Pattern** — the 2-step workflow with before/after enum snippet + full preset definition\n\n3. **Preset Button Shape Reference** — annotated example (same content, using enum key)\n\n4. **Common Mistakes** — table:\n   - Duplicate enum string value (must be globally unique)\n   - Using string literal for key instead of enum member\n   - Using string literal for `actionId` instead of an action enum value\n   - Mismatched `category` string creating an unintended new UI group\n\n5. **References** — link to `companion-preset-category-file` sibling\n\n## IMPORTANT: Overwrite the existing file completely\nThe file already exists at `.squad/skills/companion-add-preset-to-category-file/SKILL.md`. Both skills have been rewritten with the enum pattern. Here's the quick summary of what changed:

**`companion-preset-category-file`** — now mirrors the action file pattern exactly:

- Factory returns `{ [id in PresetId{Category}]: CompanionPresetExt | undefined }` (mapped type, not `{ [id: string]: ... }`)
- `presets.ts` aggregator gets the same 3 changes as `actions.ts`: import enum + factory, type local variable, extend union type — plus an `as CompanionPresetDefinitions` cast on return

**`companion-add-preset-to-category-file`** — now 2 steps (was 1):

1. Add enum member with namespaced string value
2. Add preset definition using `[PresetId{Category}.member]: { ... }` syntax
